quotes.quote: The HST images of PG 0052 + 251 and Mrk 1014 obtained with the WFC are deeper so that high surface brightness structures of extended [ O iii ] \ emission could be detected. Nevertheless, our ground-bases PMAS narrow-band images reach a lot deeper, in particular in the case of Mrk 1014 where diffuse emission is present between the high surface brightness structures. Similar IFU observation of Mrk 1014 obtained by Fu:2008 display the same [ O iii ] \ light distribution as our PMAS observation suggesting that the structures are real.

Our PMAS observation of Mrk 1014 covers only the inner 10 kpc part of the QSO host (cf. Fig. fig_pmas:EELR_det ), so that we cannot trace the bulk motion of the ionised gas across the whole galaxy. The [ O iii ] \ velocity curve along the EELR major axis appears to be peculiar. The symmetric curve has two sharp peaks in the radial velocity at 2 distance from the nucleus at which the radial motion changes from receding to approaching and vice versa. These features are associated with a region of strong jet-cloud interactions as discussed below in section pmas_sect:jet-cloud , likely leading to non-relaxed bulk motion of the gas.

sources.summary: PG0157+001 has steep VLBA and VLA spectra, multiple aligned components, and low compactness metrics, leading the paper to favor optically thin outflow emission from parsec to sub-kiloparsec scales.

quotes.quote: PG0157+001 has a steep slope with _ total = -0.9 and _ core = -0.8 , which indicates that an optically thick compact core is probably missing or below the detection limit. The steep slope emission is in agreement with earlier VLBA observations, where it was detected at 5 GHz Wang2023a , but not detected at 8.4 GHz Blundell1998 . Observations with the VLA A configuration at 1.4, 4.8, 8.4, and 45 GHz were carried out. The 4.8 and 8.4 GHz maps reveal multiple components, a central core plus one/two-sided aligned extended structures Kellermann1989,Kellermann1994,Kukula1998,Leipski2006 .

The VLA slope at 5--45 GHz is still steep with _ 5-45 = -1 Baldi2022 , which is comparable to the VLBA slope at 1--5 GHz. This suggests that the emission detected with the VLBA and the VLA, though at different scales, may have the same origin, possibly an optically thin outflow, which extends from pc to sub-kpc scales. Moreover, the large distance from the Gaia position ( 26 mas), the relatively low T_ B ( 10^ 6.4 K), the VLBA core/total flux ratio ( 0.1 ), and the VLBA/VLA flux ratio ( 0.3 ), again manifest that the emission is likely associated with an extended outflow.

sources.summary: PG 0157+001 is described as a late-stage merger and radio-quiet quasar with tidal structure, infrared excess, and steep parsec-scale jet clumps east of the optical nucleus.

quotes.quote: PG 0157+001 (Mrk 1014, z = 0.163 ) is in the late stages of a galaxy merger, characterised by a prominent tidal tail to the northeast of its host galaxy 1998ApJ...492..116S,1999MNRAS.308..377M,2000AJ....119..991S and an excess of infrared emission e.g. 1988ApJ...335L...1S . On the other hand, it has been identified as a radio-quiet quasar 1983ApJ...269..352S,1989AJ.....98.1195K , suggesting that it is in an evolutionary transition from a ULIRG to a quasar 1988ApJ...328L..35S . The radio emission of PG 0157+001 shows a triple structure along the east-west direction in the VLA map at the sub-arcsec resolution, with a total size of about 2 6 1993MNRAS.263..425M,2006A&A...455..161L .

The new 5-GHz VLBA image presented in this paper (Fig. fig:0157 b and Fig. fig:0157 ) confirms the presence of the clumps at a distance of 20--30 mas east of the optical nucleus. However, these clumps are not detected at 7.6 GHz. We have combined the 4.7 and 7.6 GHz data to produce a 6.2-GHz image, see Figure fig:0157 d , showing similar morphology to the 4.7-GHz image. The eastern clumps show an extremely steep spectral index ^ 6.2 _ 4.7 = -2.44 0.06 , consistent with optically-thin hotspots in the jet.

sources.summary: The paper presents PG 0157+001/Mrk 1014 as a warm ULIRG quasar in a merger phase, with tidal-tail host structure and VLBA radio emission offset from the optical nucleus.

quotes.quote: PG 0157+001 (Mrk 1014, z=0.163 ) is one of the brightest quasars in a class of 'warm' ultraluminous infrared galaxies (ULIRG) 1988ApJ...328L..35S,2001ApJ...554..803Y , and is in the evolutionary phase from ULIRG to ultraviolet-excess quasars 1988ApJ...335L...1S . The host galaxy of PG 0157+001 has a prominent spiral-like tidal tail extending to the northeast, indicating a recent merger 1984ApJ...283...64M . The dense star formation is restricted within the central 2 kpc region 2000AJ....120.1750C .

No VLBI component is detected near the position of the optical nucleus in our 5-GHz VLBA image. The only VLBI clump deviates from the optical nucleus by about 24 mas ( 60 pc). More data are needed to identify the nature of this feature.

sources.summary: The paper flags this AGN spectrum as a fitting exception: narrow and broad Paschen components are difficult to distinguish, so averaged best-fit line parameters are adopted.

quotes.quote: We also tried fitting the Paschen line profiles with double Gaussian profiles for objects that seem to have narrow line components (SDSSJ010226.3 003904.6, SDSSJ015530.0 085704.0, SDSSJ015950.2 + 002340.8, SDSSJ031209.2 081013.8, SDSSJ015910.0 + 010514.5, SDSSJ235156.1 010913.3, SDSSJ000943.1 090839.2 and SDSSJ011110.0 101631.8).

One exception is SDSSJ015950.2 + 002340.8 whose FWHM changed by about 50 . Distinguishing the narrow and the broad components is difficult for this object, therefore the mean value between the parameters from the two different methods was adopted as the best-fit value, with the half of the difference as its error. We note that the double component fit improved the reduced ^ 2 values significantly ( > 0.3 ) for only three objects, where the improvement came from fitting of broad extended wings which did not affect the derived fitting parameter values rather than through the change of the FWHM values (except for SDSSJ015950.2 + 002340.8).

sources.summary: The paper describes QSO 288 as a face-on spiral-hosted quasar with a same-redshift faint companion and an unusually high inferred host star-formation rate.

quotes.quote: For the QSO host galaxies in nine cases we detect [ OII ] emission. For five of them the SFR is modest ( < 1 M _ yr^ 1 ) while in two cases (QSOs 170 and 192) an high SFR was found ( 10 M_ yr^ 1 ) and in one case (QSO 288) we estimate a very high SFR ( 140 M_ yr^ 1 ).

However, in some cases (e.g the QSO 288 host; see Figure fig:ima ) the slit aperture intercepts only a fraction of the host galaxy, therefore, any gradient or structured emission may bias this measurement.
QSO 288 This QSO (z=0.1627) is hosted by a face-on spiral galaxy. The optical spectrum of the closest (object B, PD 50 kpc) faint (= 21.3) companion turned out to be at z=0.1627.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG0157+001 as a triple radio source whose western knot, bow-shock-like line emission, and spectroscopy indicate radio-jet interaction with NLR gas.

quotes.quote: PG0157+001 . This triple radio source is clearly related to the emission--line structures shown by Bennert et al. (2002) (Fig. 3). The western knot of radio emission seems to represent the termination point of a jet, which is stopped in the interstellar medium. This is strengthened by a bow--shock like structure of swept--up material in the emission--line image which is very similar to what is seen in Mrk 573 (Pogge de Robertis , Falcke et al. , Ferruit et al. ). As for Mrk 573, the interaction of the radio jet with the NLR gas producing the bow--shock is confirmed by optical spectroscopy (Leipski Bennert ).

sources.summary: The paper presents Mrk 1014 as a ULIRG outlier, excepted from the usual low-dust and AGN-dominated behavior, with infrared luminosity characteristics resembling HLIRGs.

quotes.quote: For all the galaxies of the ULIRG sample, except Mrk 1014, the low d/g clouds (m1) which significantly contribute in the LIRGs are not found, while an AGN nucleus is revealed by modelling the IR prominent flux.
In ULIRGs (Table 4) the AGN is always present (except for Mrk 1014) and dominates by 75 in Arp 220 and NGC 6240.
It seems that Mrk 1014 with log (L _ IR /L _ ) = 12.5 has characteristics similar to HLIRGs.

sources.summary: The atlas gives Mrk 1014's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1014 is a disturbed face-on ultra-luminous infrared spiral galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.1631 ( D 748 Mpc) with a radio-quiet quasar-type AGN optically classified as a Sy 1.5 veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . It features a very extended and complex NLR with two arcsecond ( 5.4 kpc; PA 90 ; e.g., bennert_size_2002 ) coinciding with the radio emission structure (e.g., leipski_radio_2006 ). After the discovery of its MIR brightness through , Mrk 1014 was monitored with ground-based N band photometry neugebauer_continuum_1987, neugebauer_variability_1999 .

sources.summary: Mrk 1014 is identified as a radio-quiet infrared-luminous QSO with broad lines and tidal tails; its mid-infrared diagnostics indicate central AGN-dominated ionization.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1014 ( z=0.1631 ) is a radio-quiet, infrared luminous QSO with broad optical emission lines and twin tidal tails indicative of a recent interaction (MacKenty Stockton 1984).
Mrk 1014 has a steeply rising mid-infrared spectrum with weak emission features and little or no silicate absorption.

The [ NeV ] 14.3 / [ NeII ] 12.8 and [ OIV ] 25.9 / [ NeII ] 12.8 line flux ratios in Mrk 1014 (0.9 and 1.7, respectively) suggests that nearly all the ionizing flux comes from the central AGN, although the obvious presence of PAH emission suggests some extranuclear star formation.

gmw_00000150.name: Mrk 1018
gmw_00000150.overview: Mrk 1018 is a post-merger lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770, arXiv:2307.14139] featuring an irregular host with a tidal tail [arXiv:2307.14139] and active star formation [arXiv:1303.4084]. Its active nucleus is a changing-look Seyfert [arXiv:2003.08641, arXiv:2304.09435] that has transitioned multiple times between spectral types 1 and 1.9 [arXiv:2003.08641, arXiv:2306.01036].

sources.summary: The paper reports that the changing-look AGN Mrk 1018 returned to a Seyfert 1.9 state, with dimmed broad lines and no evidence for increased reddening.

quotes.quote: Draft as of We report the discovery that the known `changing look' AGN Mrk 1018 has changed spectral type for a second time. New VLT-MUSE data taken in 2015 as part of the Close AGN Reference Survey (CARS) shows that the AGN has returned to its original Seyfert 1.9 classification. The CARS sample is selected to contain only bright type 1 AGN, but Mrk 1018's broad emission lines and continuum, typical of type 1 AGN, have almost entirely disappeared. We use spectral fitting of the MUSE spectrum and previously available spectra to determine the drop in broad line flux and the Balmer decrement.

In this letter we presented evidence for the AGN Mrk 1018 returning to its Seyfert 1.9 state after 30 years as a Seyfert 1. Using MUSE data from the Close AGN Reference Survey alongside archival spectroscopic and photometric observations we explored the possible causes of this change: a decline in accretion rate or lack of fuel, a TDE, obscuration, or disruption of the accretion disk. We reason that the length and consistency of Mrk 1018's bright phase makes a TDE an unlikely explanation, but we cannot rule out a simple decline in accretion rate. The Balmer decrement between the Seyfert 1 and 1.9 phases implies that the obscuration along our line of sight has not increased, but a highly opaque column of gas selectively obscuring our view is still a possibility.

sources.summary: The paper reports that the changing-look AGN Mrk 1018 stopped dimming, briefly rebrightened, and showed broad-line asymmetry that motivates continued multiwavelength monitoring.

quotes.quote: After changing optical AGN type from 1.9 to 1 in 1984, the AGN Mrk 1018 recently reverted back to its type 1.9 state. Our ongoing monitoring now reveals that the AGN has halted its dramatic dimming, reaching a minimum around October 2016. The minimum was followed by an outburst rising with 0.25 U-band mag/month. The rebrightening lasted at least until February 2017, as confirmed by joint Chandra and Hubble observations. Monitoring was resumed in July 2017 after the source emerged from sunblock, at which point the AGN was found only 0.4 mag brighter than its minimum.

The intermittent outburst was accompanied by the appearance of a red wing asymmetry in broad-line shape, indicative of an inhomogeneous broad-line region. The current flickering brightness of Mrk 1018 following its rapid fading either suggests that the source has reignited, remains variable at a low level, or may continue dimming over the next few years. Distinguishing between these possibilities requires continuous multiwavelength monitoring.

sources.summary: The paper models Mrk 1018 as a changing-look Seyfert whose repeated type changes and recent dimming motivate predicted polarization echoes for future monitoring.

quotes.quote: To support such eagerly awaited observations, we selected the best-documented case, the changing-look Seyfert galaxy Mrk 1018, and applied our numerical tool to make quantitative predictions for a future observing campaign. Mrk 1018 is a type 1 AGN situated at a Hubble distance of 183.7 Mpc (heliocentric redshift = 0.04244). It is a peculiar target because it is one of the few low-luminosity AGNs (Seyfert galaxy) known to have transitioned many times across the spectral sequence. Mrk 1018 varied from type 1.9 to 1 between 1979 September and 1984 January Cohen1986 , then returned again to type 1.9 in 2015 McElroy2016 .

We modeled Mrk 1018 using the same general AGN geometry as in Sect. Model . We fixed the host galaxy contribution during the bright (pre-2011) and dim (post-2016) states of Mrk 1018 using the luminosity ratio of the nucleus to the host galaxy as a function of epoch presented in Kim2018 . The authors analyzed multi-epoch Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) g-band and SWIFT B-band imaging data and derived a luminosity fraction L _ AGN /L _ host 0.4 in the bright state and 0.03 in the dim state. We allowed the nucleus to decreases its flux by a factor 25, such as observed by McElroy2016 .

sources.summary: The paper focuses on Mrk 1018, finding a compact variable flat-spectrum radio core whose apparent proper motion is more consistent with an unresolved jet than a recoiling SMBH.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1018 is a nearby changing-look AGN that has oscillated between spectral Type 1.9 and Type 1 over a period of 40 years.

In this paper, we present an analysis of 6.5 years of multi-frequency Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) monitoring of Mrk 1018. We find that the radio emission is compact down to 2.4 pc, and displays flux density and spectral variability over the length of our campaign, typical of a flat spectrum radio core. We observe proper motion in RA of the radio core at -36.4 8.6 as yr ^ 1 (4.2 ), or 0.10c 0.02c at the redshift of Mrk 1018. No significant proper motion is found in DEC (31.3 25.1 as yr ^ 1 ).

We conclude that the apparent velocity we observe of the VLBI radio core is too high to reconcile with theoretical predictions of rSMBH velocities and that the proper motion is most likely dominated by an unresolved, outflowing jet component. Future observations may yet reveal the true nature of Mrk 1018. However, our observations are not able to confirm it as a true rSMBH.

sources.summary: The paper models Mrk 1018 as a changing-look AGN whose observed low/high optical-UV states and 5- to 20-year transition timescales can be reproduced by a magnetic disk-outflow model.

quotes.quote: The calculated spectra of the disk with outflows can fit the observed spectra of a CL AGN Mrk 1018 quite well both in the low and high states. The derived inflow time of such a disk with outflows is around 5 years in the high state, while it becomes 20 years in the low state, which is roughly consistent with the observations of the variabilities in Mrk 1018.

We further apply our model to a well observed CL AGN Mrk 1018( z=0.035 , of which the BH mass is estimated as log(M_ BH /M_ )=7.4-7.9 . The Eddington ratios are L/L_ Edd =0.03 and L/L_ Edd =0.004 in the high and low states, respectively see and the references therein 2016A&A...593L...8M,2016A&A...593L...9H,2018MNRAS.480.3898N . Mrk 1018 changed from type 1.9 to type 1 in 5 years 1986ApJ...311..135C,1989ApJ...340..190G . 2016A&A...593L...8M 's observations show that Mrk 1018 changed from type 1 to type 1.9 after about 30 years. We adopt the black hole mass M_ BH =10^8 M_ in our model calculations for Mrk 1018.

sources.summary: The paper uses Mrk 1018 as a changing-look AGN with steep Balmer-decrement behavior, low absorption, and SED evolution requiring truncated-disk models in low states.

quotes.quote: The H /H in some optical CLAGNs showing the appearance and disappearance of broad emission lines can reach up to 10 or even higher with a decrease of luminosities ( e.g., H /H =8-15 at 150 days after the transient in 1ES 1927+654, Trakhtenbrot2019 ; H /H =8-10 in NGC 2992, Guolo2021 ; H /H =4-6 in NGC 3516, Shapovalova2019 ; H /H =4-9 in Mrk 1018, Cohen1986,McElroy2016 ). The effects of collisional excitation and/or dust extinction are suggested to be the reason for such a large deviation of the Balmer decrement .

Mrk 1018 shows strong evolution of SEDs in four quasi-simultaneous observations from optical to X-ray e.g., and references therein Noda2018,Lyu2021 . To build the SED for each observation, we fit the optical spectrum with SSD or truncated SSD model and fit the X-ray spectrum with a power-law component, where we simply assume the cutoff at log = 19.5 . We only fit four optical data points in optical-UV bands because the contribution from a galaxy is not important in these bands. For two bright states, the SSD can roughly reproduce the optical spectra, while two low-state observations need the truncated SSD with a transition radius of 70 R_ g and 110 R_ g , respectively (see the top-left panel of Figure fig3 ).
More importantly, the absorption is always weak in some CLAGNs e.g. Mrk 1018, LaMassa2017 during the changing look stage.

sources.summary: The paper follows Mrk 1018 after its changing-look transition, ruling out obscuration and attributing the fading primarily to a declining accretion-disc luminosity.

quotes.quote: We recently discovered that the active galactic nucleus (AGN) of Mrk 1018 has changed optical type again after 30 years as a type 1 AGN. Here we combine Chandra , NuStar , Swift , Hubble Space Telescope and ground-based observations to explore the cause of this change. The 2--10 keV flux declines by a factor of 8 between 2010 and 2016.

We show with our X-ray observation that this is not caused by varying neutral hydrogen absorption along the line-of-sight up to the Compton-thick level. The optical-UV spectral energy distributions are well fit with a standard geometrically thin optically thick accretion disc model that seems to obey the expected L T^4 relation. It confirms that a decline in accretion disc luminosity is the primary origin for the type change.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes changing-look AGN Mrk 1018, linking multiwavelength fading, re-flaring, X-ray/optical lags, radio decline, and type-state correlations to accretion-disk evolution.

quotes.quote: We explore this issue based on the multi-wavelength spectral and flux variations for a changing-look AGN Mrk 1018 with long-term observations in the X-ray, optical/ultraviolet(UV), and radio bands. Both the optical and the X-ray emission experience rapid decay in changing-look phase during 2010--2015, where a re-flare appears in the optical/UV and X-ray bands.

We find a time lag of 20 days of optical/UV behind X-ray variations in type 1.9 phase. The 5 GHz radio flux decreases by 20 in type 1.9 phase during 2016--2017. We find both X-ray photon index ( ) and the optical-to-X-ray spectral index ( ) are anti-correlated with the Eddington scaled 2--10 keV X-ray luminosity ( L_ X /L_ Edd ) in the type 1.9 phase.

However, the type 1 phase deviates from these two anti-correlations, which suggests that the change of broad emission lines might be regulated by the evolution of accretion disk (e.g., disappearing of the inner cold disk in the type 1.9 phase).

sources.summary: The paper presents Mrk 1018 as a post-merger changing-look Seyfert system with an irregular, tidal-tailed host and repeated spectral-state transitions.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1018 is a galaxy and AGN system with a redshift of 0.043 veron-cetty . It is a post-merger remnant: the host galaxy is clearly irregular, with a tidal tail mcelroy . It was first classified as a Seyfert 1.9 galaxy by a spectrum taken in 1979 and published in osterbrock , with a broader H than expected in a classical Seyfert 2 galaxy.

The changing-look nature of the AGN was then revealed via a 1984 spectrum published in cohen . The optical emission lines, including H , were found to have broadened and the luminosity had significantly increased, reclassifying Mrk 1018 as a Seyfert 1 galaxy. The AGN remained in this bright state for approximately 30 years before a serendipitous Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) observation that found the AGN mid-transition -- in the process of returning to its former type 1.9 state mcelroy .

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 1597 as a bright S0 outlier, a Seyfert galaxy with an active core, disturbed merger-like appearance, extreme blue colours, strong central H emission, and star formation.

quotes.quote: One bright S0 galaxy lies away from the main group towards the lower left side of the figure. This is UGC 1597, which is a Seyfert galaxy with an active core and a disturbed appearance in the SDSS image, indicative of a recent merger. These peculiarities explain its extreme blue colours. The SDSS fiber spectrum of the center of this galaxy shows very strong H emission indicating that this is a strongly star forming galaxy. We exclude this galaxy from the sample that is analysed in subsequent subsections.

sources.summary: The CARS paper discusses Mrk 1018 as a changing-look AGN used to test ENLR size scaling, with its black-hole mass taken from the bright phase.

quotes.quote: In addition to our discovery of a prominent changing look (CLAGN) event in Mrk 1018 McElroy:2016,Husemann:2016b,Krumpe:2017 within CARS, we performed a systematic study of star formation within the bars of the galaxies presented by Neumann:2019 , which indicates differences in star formation rates that are likely related to secular evolution rather than AGN feedback. Luckily, the CARS sample contains 2 well-known changing-look AGN, namely HE 0212 0059 aka Mrk 590 Osterbrock:1977,Denney:2014 and HE 0203 0019 aka Mrk 1018 Cohen:1986,McElroy:2016,Krumpe:2017 , which we use here to test the impact on the ENLR size scaling.

We include both changing-look AGN as stripes in Fig. fig:ENLR_size_overview representing the range in AGN luminosity from the observed maximum to 1/100 and 1/10 of the luminosity as observed for Mrk 590 Denney:2014 and Mrk 1018 McElroy:2016,Krumpe:2017 , respectively, during their minimum state. As shown in Fig. fig:ENLR_MBH_relation , both changing-look AGN Mrk 1018 and Mrk 590 are in agreement with the ENLR size--BH mass relation. For Mrk 590 we use the broad H parameters as measured from the MUSE IFU data during a recent rebrightening phase, and for Mrk 1018 we used the BH mass estimated during its bright phase Bennert:2015 as the broad H line remained too faint after the last changing-look event.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 1018 as a local active galaxy in its bare-AGN sample and analyzes XMM-Newton/Swift X-ray spectra, including continuum, soft-excess, and spectral-fitting behavior.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1018 is a Seyfert AGN which is mostly popular for its changing-look behaviour. It changed its behaviour from Seyfert 1.9 to Seyfert 1 between 1979 and 1984 Cohen1986 and then returned to its previous state in 2015 McElroy2016 . The AGN is located at a redshift of z =0.0424 and the central black hole mass is estimated as 6.91 10^7 Ezhikode2017 . Mrk 1018 was observed with XMM-Newton and Swift /XRT at multiple epochs. The details of the observation log are given in Table table:obs_log .

sources.summary: The paper describes J0206 0017 as a radio AGN with compact unresolved emission and a curved, flattened GHz spectrum.

quotes.quote: J0206 0017 is a radio AGN associated with the changing-look AGN Mrk 1018 McElroy+16,Walsh+23 . The radio emission is unresolved by RACS, FIRST, and at 3 GHz and 10 GHz (Figure fig:0206 ). We note that the local image RMS of this RACS field is 450 Jy, and thus the compact nature of J0206 0017's 888 MHz radio emission is not truly reflected in Figure fig:0206 . The radio spectral index reflects the compact nature of this source, as it is flat with _3^ 10 =-0.13 0.04 . The broadband radio SED is well fit by a curved power law and appears to be inverted ( q=0.29 0.01 ).

sources.summary: The paper treats Mrk 1018 as a changing-look AGN with low intrinsic absorption, strong long-term X-ray variation, and spectral-index evolution across an Eddington-ratio transition.

quotes.quote: However, some other CL AGNs cannot be explained by the obscuration scenario since that its intrinsic absorption is always low e.g., Mrk 1018, mcel16 and the low level of polarization in some CL AGNs e.g., huts19 . nd18 proposed that the CL AGNs are possibly triggered by the rapid drop or increase of accretion rate based on the broadband spectrum of a CL AGN (Mrk 1018) and it may be similar to the state transition in stellar-mass X-ray binaries (XRBs).

The archived data for both NuSTAR, XMM-Newton and Chandra are selected and reduced for four famous CL AGNs -- Mrk 1018, NGC 3516, Mrk 590 and NGC 2992, where these sources suffer strong X-ray variations in last 20 years. For Mrk 1018, the X-ray spectral index show strong evolution, where and L_ 2-10 keV /L_ Edd follow positive and negative correlations for L_ 2-10 keV /L_ Edd 10^ 3 and 10^ 3 respectively.

sources.summary: The atlas gives Mrk 1018's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1018 is a lenticular galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.0424 ( D 191 Mpc) with an active nucleus that belongs to the nine-month BAT AGN sample. It was initially optically classified as a Sy 1.9 but intrinsically changed to Sy 1.0 type in 1984 cohen_variability_1986,trippe_multi-wavelength_2010 . At radio wavelengths, the nucleus appears unresolved at subarcsecond resolution ulvestad_radio_1986 .

sources.summary: The paper uses Mrk 1018 as a changing-look Seyfert example whose optical-to-X-ray SED supports an intrinsic tenfold accretion-power drop rather than variable obscuration.

quotes.quote: In addition, in the Seyfert galaxy Mrk 1018 a full optical to X-ray SED shows no sign of variable obscuration, but is rather fully consistent with a drop in intrinsic accretion power by a factor 10 mcelroyetal2016,husemannetal2016 over a few years.
The red stars schematically show the changing look AGN Mrk 1018, assuming that the variations are sourced at the optical emission radius R = 50 r_g .
However, this seems disfavored by energetics in Mrk 1018 due to the low X-ray/UV luminosity husemannetal2016 .

sources.summary: The paper summarizes Mrk 1018 as a known changing-look AGN that turned on, later turned off, and showed an X-ray flux drop associated with the optical transition.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1018 Mrk 1018 (BAT 106) was observed to turn on between 1979 and 1984 1986ApJ...311..135C , and then turn off again between 2009 and 2015 2016A&A...593L...8M, 2016A&A...593L...9H . In the X-rays, there is a drop in flux from 2009 to 2015 which we associate with the turn-off CS event seen in the optical. Mrk 1018 was observed by SDSS in 2000 September with a type 1 spectrum which was subsequently included in BASS DR1 and DR2.

gmw_00000151.name: Mrk 1239
gmw_00000151.overview: Mrk 1239 is an elliptical or lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770, arXiv:1501.02022] hosting a highly polarized narrow-line Seyfert 1 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2105.13672, arXiv:2401.07676] that accretes near the Eddington limit [arXiv:2105.02078, arXiv:2209.14410]. The system is viewed almost face-on [arXiv:2105.02078, arXiv:2405.08785] and features a parsec-scale radio jet [arXiv:2111.12980] alongside a bright, extensive emission-line region [arXiv:1501.02022].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 1239 as an NLS1 with FR I-like extended emission, missing VLBA flux, broad diffuse emission around the core, and a core-dominated flat spectrum.

quotes.quote: J0952-0136 Mrk 1239 is a NLS1 which has been classified as radio-quiet Doi15 or barely radio-loud Berton15a . This object was studied repeatedly in radio, finding extended emission reminiscent of an FR I radio galaxy Doi13, Doi15 . Comparing VLA and VLBA observations, Orienti10 found that almost 80 of the VLA (at 15 GHz) flux was missing on VLBA scales, suggesting the presence of a very significant extended emission. Our map, shown in Fig. fig:J0952m0136 , reveals indeed extended emission everywhere around the core, which was the only component visible in 15 GHz VLA images. The largest extension is measured at PA 212 ^ , at a projected distance of 3.7 arcsec (1.5 kpc).

The unresolved core has a size of 84 33 pc, and it shows a flat in-band spectrum. The total emission also has a flat spectrum, but it is dominated by the core, which represents the 90 of the entire flux. The broad-band spectrum is instead steep between 1.4 and 5 GHz and, as expected from this slope, the source is not detected at 22 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper models Mrk 1239 as a nearby narrow-line Seyfert 1 with very soft X-ray continuum emission, near-Eddington accretion, and a high-covering-factor torus viewed close to face-on.

quotes.quote: Suzaku observed Mrk 1239 in 2007 for 63 ks (obs ID: 702031010).

We present torus modelling for the X-ray spectra of a nearby narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy \ ( z=0.0199 ), based on archival , \ and \ observations. Our model suggests very soft intrinsic power-law continuum emission of 2.57 in 2019 and 2.98 in 2007. By applying a correction factor to the unabsorbed X-ray luminosity, we find that \ is accreting near or around the Eddington limit . Our best-fit spectral model also suggests a torus with a column density of (N_ H, ave / cm ^ 2 )=25.0 0.2 and a high covering factor of 0.90 in , indicating that this source is most likely to be viewed almost face-on with i 26^ .

The primary X-ray continuum of \ is described by a power law with slope =2.6-3.0 . Such a soft continuum suggests that \ is one of the most extreme AGNs that are accreting near or around the Eddington limit. By applying a correction factor to its X-ray luminosity, we obtain _ Edd =0.1-2.8 after taking into account the uncertainty of the BH mass measurements.

sources.summary: Mrk 1239 is described as a polarized narrow-line Seyfert 1 with a radio jet-like structure; GRAVITY finds weak BLR phase signals but geometry consistent with a rotating BLR.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1239 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLSy1) galaxy well known for its high polarisation Goodrich1989, Smith2004 , relatively redder optical-IR colour compared to other typical NLSy1s, and presence of a radio jet-like structure Orienti2010, Doi2015 oriented perpendicular to the polarisation angle. Mrk 1239 has not been a target for RM campaigns, but previous works have estimated its BH mass and BLR size via indirect estimates from various scaling relations. They all point to the same conclusion that Mrk 1239 has a small but uncertain BH mass, M_ BH 10^ 5.7 10^ 7.0 \ M_ e.g. Kaspi2005, Greene2005, Ryan2007, Du2014a, Buhariwalla2020, Pan2021, GRAVITY2023b . Its BLR size is also estimated to be R_ BLR 10 to 20 ld e.g. Du2014a, GRAVITY2023b .

The red- and blueshifted photocentres of Mrk 1239 show an insignificant separation (see Fig. fig:photocentre_complete ). The photocentre fitting results of Mrk 1239 suggest that the differential phase is dominated by the continuum phase, and the BLR differential phase is very moderate (low S/N). However, the perpendicular orientation of the BLR velocity gradient and continuum photocentre and the radio jet is consistent with the picture of a rotating BLR. Despite the relatively weak signal of Mrk 1239, we argue that this aids in adding further constraint to our BLR fitting compared to when we only fit its flux spectrum (as proven in Sect. sec:visphi_importance ).

sources.summary: The paper presents deep X-ray observations of Mrk 1239, interpreting a strong flare, absorbed continuum, soft emission components, and face-on geometry in a complex NLS1 environment.

quotes.quote: A Hot Mess: Mrk 1239 Buhariwalla et al. 2024 Previous X-ray works on \ have revealed a complex Narrow Line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) that exhibits substantial absorption and strong emission from both collisional (CIE) and photoionized (PIE) plasmas. Here, we report on deep-pointed observations with \ and , along with \ monitoring, to understand the 0.3-30 \ continuum emission and the central engine geometry. A strong X-ray flare, where the AGN brightens by a factor of five in 30 , is captured between 4-30 \ and can be attributed to a brightening of the primary continuum. However, the lack of any variability below 3 \ on long- or short-time scales requires complete absorption of the AGN continuum with a neutral medium of column density 10^ 23.5 . The timing and spectral properties are consistent with a blurred reflection interpretation for the primary emission.

In this work, we estimate the inclination of the accretion disc to be 17^ , based on the blurred reflection model. The inclination of the torus was measured to be 6^ Lakicevic+2022 , which was consistent with the measurements of inclination of the broad line region ( 7^ , Zhang+2002 ; 11^ , Gravity+2024 ). We conclude that we are observing the system virtually face-on.

sources.summary: The paper reports VLBA evidence for a compact parsec-scale bipolar jet in the highly accreting radio-quiet NLS1 galaxy Mrk 1239 and discusses its radio-emission origin.

quotes.quote: In recent years, the work dedicated to study parsec-scale region of the radio-quiet NLS1 galaxies has found jet features in several of them 2013ApJ...765...69D , but only Mrk 1239 has a reliably measured Eddington ratio close to/higher than one Another one is Ark 564, the VLBI image of which shows an elongated structure as reported by 2004A&A...425...99L . However, it is noted in their paper that the ( u , v ) coverage obtained was relatively poor, and they do not feel confident that the elongated feature is an accurate representation of the VLBI-scale structure of the source. 2015ApJ...798L..30D, 2021ApJ...912..118P . So further studies on the parsec-scale nuclear region in radio-quiet NLS1 galaxies with highest Eddington ratios should provide general clues of the interplay between the jet and accretion disk. Our observations will not only search the signature of jet in these radio-quiet highly accreting systems, but also extend our knowledge of the parsec-scale radio properties of AGN to a rarely explored parameter space.

At parsec scales, we have resolved \ at 1.5 GHz into an elongated structure. The two dimensional Gaussian model fitting to the visibility data has revealed five components distributed along the elongated structure, among which ``S1'' and ``S2'' components have T_ B >10^ 7 K, indicating the non-thermal emission from jet. This provides a strong evidence for a parsec-scale jet launched by the SMBH in .

sources.summary: The paper uses continuum-based dust measurements to infer BLR and black-hole properties for Mrk 1239, finding strong deviation from the radius-luminosity relation and near-Eddington accretion.

quotes.quote: Four targets, Mrk 1239, Mrk 231, IRAS 09149 6206, and PDS 456, show the most significant deviation from the relation. As discussed in Section ssec:mass , all of them, except for IRAS 09149 6206, are at or above or above their Eddington luminosity. The Eddington ratio of IRAS 09149 6206 (about 0.4) is also high among the typical AGNs (e.g. the rest of the sample). Thus the large deviations from the \ relation could be related to their high accretion rates Du2019 .

Four AGNs, Mrk 1239, Mrk 231, IRAS 13349+2438, and PDS 456, are close to or above the Eddington accretion. Their optical spectra (see their references in Col. (10) of Table tab:smp ) commonly show features of narrow widths of the broad \ lines, weak or no \ lines, and strong \ features. These features strongly indicate that these targets have high accretion rates ( Boroson1992,Shen2014 and references therein), consistent with our Eddington ratios.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 1239 as a well studied NLS1 with a compact 5.5 GHz core, intermediate radio luminosity, small black-hole mass, steep radio spectrum, and extended higher-resolution emission.

quotes.quote: J0952 0136 (Mrk 1239) Mrk 1239 is a well studied object in literature. It exhibits a very compact core with the C-configuration at 5.5 GHz (Fig. o right panel) and has a flux density of S_ int 21.28 mJy. The radio luminosity is at an intermediate level with L_ int 39.01. The black hole mass is relatively small with (M_ BH / M_ ) 6.33, which is consistent with other measurements in the range of (M_ BH / M_ ) 5.89-7.02 Ryan2007,Berton2015 . The radio spectrum is steep with _ in-band 0.91.

sources.summary: The paper argues that Mrk 1239 is probably a type-2 counterpart of NLS1s, based on high polarization, red optical-infrared colors, and a radio jet-like structure.

quotes.quote: Similar to many other type-2s, jet-like structure in the radio band is found in Mrk 1239, perpendicular to the polarization angle, suggesting polar scattering. We argue that Mrk 1239 is very probably a type-2 counterpart of NLS1s.
High P value of Mrk 1239 was also found in our spectropolarimetric data. The intrinsic P of Mrk 1239 is then P>10 in the optical band (Figure 2()), increasing with decreasing wavelength.

The high degree of polarization and very red optical-IR colors in Mrk 1239 are clearly contradicting its original identification as a type-1 AGN, making it an extremely interesting object in the framework of the AGN unifcation.

sources.summary: The paper presents near-infrared spectroscopy of Mrk 1239, finding a uniquely strong 2.2 m nuclear bump, heavy reddening, and a 1200 K blackbody excess attributed to hot dust near the AGN.

quotes.quote: We report 0.8 4.5 m SpeX spectroscopy of the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy Mrk 1239. The spectrum is outstanding because the nuclear continuum emission in the near-infrared is dominated by a strong bump of emission peaking at 2.2 m, with a strength not reported before in an AGN. A comparison of the Mrk 1239 spectrum to that of Ark 564 allowed us to conclude that the continuum is strongly reddened by E(B-V)=0.54. The excess of emission, confirmed by aperture photometry and additional NIR spectroscopy, follows a simple blackbody curve at T 1200 K. This suggest that we may be observing direct evidence of dust heated near to the sublimation temperature, likely produced by the putative torus of the unification model.

sources.summary: The paper discusses J0952-0136 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio and spectral-index properties, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J0952-0136 J0952-0136 (Mrk 1239) is a radio-quiet NLS1 at z = 0.020 that has been widely studied in the past. It has one of the highest flux densities in our sample, the peak flux density is 17.49 mJy beam ^ 1 , the integrated flux density is 18.54 mJy, and the flux densities of the tapered maps about 0.5 mJy higher. Due to its low redshift this translates to a moderate luminosity of log L_ , int = 39.04 erg s ^ 1 . Our radio map (Fig. fig:J0952spind ) shows a compact component and almost symmetrical extended emission toward north-east and south-west at a position angle of 32 .

sources.summary: The paper says Mrk 1239 has complex NLSy1 X-ray behavior: blurred reflection, ionized partial covering, starburst-dominated soft emission, and inconclusive absorption testing in their model.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1239 Bu2020,Bu2023 report a very detailed analysis on this NLSy1. The hard band requires a relativistic blurred reflection component besides the power law which is partially covered by ionized material. This source has starburst activity, which affects and even dominates the soft band. Since we do not account for this features, our model fails to fit the continuum and the absorption test results inconclusive.

sources.summary: The atlas treats Mrk 1239 as an individually observed active or starburst galaxy, summarizing its prior classification and near-infrared continuum, emission-line, and absorption properties.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1239 . Classified as a NLS1 galaxy by oter85 , this object displays a highly polarized optical spectrum goo89 and one of the steepest X-ray spectra found in AGNs, with _x =+3.0 based on ROSAT PSPC data gmk04 . sm04 modeled the polarization nature of this object and find that it is one of the rare cases of Seyfert 1 galaxies that appear to be dominated by scattering in an extended region along the poles of the torus. The continuum emission of this source is the most outstanding of all the objects in our sample. The detailed study by rom06 shows that the NIR is dominated by a strong bump of emission peaking at 2.2 m, with a strength not reported before in an AGN. The bump follows a simple blackbody curve at T 1200 K.

sources.summary: The atlas gives Mrk 1239's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1239 is an elliptical galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.0199 ( D 88 Mpc) with an AGN classified as a Sy 1.5 and Sy 1n veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . This object has a steep radio spectrum with an unresolved core ulvestad_radio_1995 and an extended NLR (diameter 10 4.1 kpc; PA 11 ; mulchaey_emission-line_1996 ). After , ground-based MIR observations of Mrk 1239 were performed by .

sources.summary: MARK 1239 is an E-S0 galaxy with a luminous Seyfert 1 nucleus, rich high-excitation coronal spectrum, and bright extensive elliptical ENLR.

quotes.quote: MARK 1239: This E-S0 galaxy contains a very luminous Seyfert 1 with a very rich and high-excitation coronal spectrum including [ Fe 14 ] see Figure fig:fig4 . The ENLR is very bright and extensive with an elliptical morphology ( 17 15 arcsec, 7.1 6.3 kpc). There is no sign of any \ regions.

gmw_00000152.name: NGC 34
gmw_00000152.overview: NGC 34 is a luminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:1010.0948, arXiv:2107.09731] representing a gas-rich merger remnant [arXiv:astro-ph/0702645, arXiv:1010.0948] with tidal tails [arXiv:astro-ph/0702645, arXiv:astro-ph/9811342] and a single coalesced nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0702645, arXiv:1406.6345]. It hosts a strong central starburst [arXiv:astro-ph/0702645, arXiv:1010.0948] alongside an obscured Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1810.02371, arXiv:2107.10855] that drives a high-velocity galactic wind [arXiv:2002.10812, arXiv:2107.09731].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 34 as a gas-rich merger remnant with a single nucleus, blue central disk, tidal tails, young clusters, a strong starburst, and a late gaseous outflow.

quotes.quote: This paper presents new images and spectroscopic observations of 34 (Mrk 938) obtained with the du Pont 2.5-m and Baade 6.5-m telescopes at Las Campanas, plus photometry of an archival V image obtained with Hubble Space Telescope . This M_V = -21.6 galaxy has often been classified as a Seyfert 2, yet recently published infrared spectra suggest a dominant central starburst. We find that the galaxy features a single nucleus, a main spheroid containing a blue central disk and much outer fine structure, and tidal tails indicative of two former disk galaxies. At present these galaxies appear to have completed merging. The remnant shows three clear optical signs that the merger was gas-rich (``wet'') and accompanied by a starburst: (1) It sports a rich system of young star clusters, of which 87 have absolute magnitudes 10.0 M_V 15.4 .

Assessing all available evidence, we suggest that 34 stems from two recently merged gas-rich disk galaxies with an estimated mass ratio of 1/3 m/M 2/3 . The remnant seems to have first experienced a galaxy-wide starburst that then shrank to its current central and obscured state. The strong gaseous outflow came last.

sources.summary: NGC 34 is treated as an advanced major-merger Seyfert LIRG with compact nuclear mid-infrared emission and unusual silicate/H2 behavior that prevents a reliable extinction correction.

quotes.quote: Three of the galaxies (NGC 34, IRASF 05189--2524, and UGC 05101) are advanced major mergers.
Likewise, most of the mid-IR emission of NGC 34 and NGC 7469 is produced in the compact nuclear region (few kpc, Esquej2012, Diaz-Santos2010b ), thus no aperture matching corrections are needed.

NGC 34 has a strong silicate absorption ( S_ Si =-1.10 ) and an observed 0--0 S(1) 0--0 S(3) =0.77 0.07 , which is compatible with the average extinction corrected ratio of the other galaxies. Therefore, it is possible that the regions producing the \ and 10 \ continuum emissions in NGC 34 suffer from very different extinction levels. Moreover, the NGC 34 uncorrected \ SLED does not have any indication of attenuation of the 0--0 S(3) transition (see Figure fig:powerlaw_fit ). This is at odds with the UGC 05101 ( S_ Si =--1.50) SLED that shows a clear deficit of 0--0 S(3) emission. As a consequence, we do not apply any correction to NGC 34 since we do not have an accurate measurement of extinction toward the \ emitting regions.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 34 as a current LIRG with older massive clusters, inferring a stronger past starburst, ULIRG-level peak luminosity, and a long LIRG/ULIRG phase.

quotes.quote: One galaxy, NGC 34, stands out as unique in the sample assembled here. It is currently a LIRG with a far-IR luminosity of 10 ^ 11.44 , which translates to a SFR of 75-90 (S03; Prouton et al. 2004). However, it appears to have had a higher SFR in the past. Schweizer Seitzer (2007) have obtained spectroscopic ages of the two brightest clusters ( =-15.36 ) in the galaxy, and have derived ages of 150 Myr (although they note that cluster 2 may be 4 times older).

Therefore, we can infer that the SFR and have both been declining for the past 150 Myr to the current levels, and that this timescale is a lower limit to the duration of the LIRG/ULIRG phase. If cluster 2 is indeed four times older, then the ULIRG duration for this galaxy is at least 600 Myr, or if the SFR has not been continuous, then NGC 34 has gone through multiple ULIRG phases.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 34 as a gas-rich wet merger remnant with tidal-tail H I, a central starburst, weak AGN activity, and luminous infrared emission.

quotes.quote: We present VLA observations of the neutral hydrogen and radio continuum of NGC 34 (= NGC 17 = Mrk 938). This object is an ideal candidate to study the fate of gas in mergers, since, as shown by an optical study done by SS07 , it is a gas-rich (``wet'') merger remnant of two disk galaxies of unequal mass hosting a strong central starburst and a weak AGN. We detect HI emission from both tidal tails and from nearby galaxies, suggesting that NGC 34 is actually part of a gas-rich group and might have recently interacted with one of its companions.

Single-dish radio data show NGC 34 to be gas-rich in both neutral and molecular hydrogen, since the neutral-hydrogen mass is M_ HI 1.7 10^ 10 M_ MW84 , and the molecular-gas mass derived from CO observations is M_ H_2 7 3 10^9 M_ K03,K90,C92 . NGC 34 has also been studied extensively in the infrared, since it is a luminous infrared galaxy (LIRG).
The infrared luminosity of NGC 34 is (L_ IR /L_ ) = 11.61 C92 , suggesting that both the starburst and the AGN contribute significantly to it.

sources.summary: NGC 34 is treated as a gas-rich NGC 17/Mrk 938 merger remnant hosting starburst and weak AGN activity; CO and H I observations reveal a compact rotating central gas disk.

quotes.quote: NGC 34 ( = NGC 17 = Mrk 938) is an ideal candidate to study these processes since it is gas-rich and hosts both a starburst and an AGN. Throughout the paper, we adopt a distance of 85.2 Mpc, derived for H_0 = 70 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 by hereafter SS07 SS07 . The infrared luminosity of this object is (L_ IR /L_ )=11.61 Chini92 , placing it in mid-range of the classically defined Luminous Infrared Galaxies (LIRGs; 11.0 < L_ IR /L_ < 12.0 ; ). In general, high IR luminosities are due to dust grains being heated by a combination of starburst and AGN, with higher values indicating a greater AGN contribution SandersMirabel96 . The IR luminosity of NGC 34 suggests that the starburst is the dominant phenomenon in the center, with a minor AGN component Goncalves99,Prouton04 .

We present CO(1-0) and (21-cm) observations of the central region of the wet merger remnant NGC 34. The Combined Array for Research in Millimeter-wave Astronomy (CARMA) observations detect a regularly rotating disk in CO with a diameter of 2.1 kpc and a total molecular hydrogen mass of ( 2.1 0.2) 10^9 M_ . The rotation curve of this gas disk rises steeply, reaching maximum velocities at 1 \ (410 pc) from the center.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 34 as a luminous infrared merger system whose unequal gas-rich progenitors produced a galaxy-wide starburst, young globular clusters, a blue disk, coalesced nuclei, and strong gaseous outflow.

quotes.quote: NGC 34: at a distance of 78.4 Mpc, this LIRG was classified as a merger system by vv59 .

Using optical images and spectroscopic observations sch07 proposed a scenario about the merging history of NGC 34. They suggested that two disk gas-rich galaxies of unequal mass (with an estimated mas ratio of 1/3 m/M 2/3) merged, yielding a galaxy-wide starburst. This starburst occurred first 600 Myr (showing a peak over 100 Myr ago) and, according to the authors, seems to have formed an extensive system of young globular clusters with ages in the range of 0.1 to 1.0 Gyr. This work also reveals a young, blue stellar exponential disk that formed 400 Myr ago. At present, the two merging galaxies' nuclei appear to have coalesced, the starburst has shrunk to its current central ( 1 kpc) state and there is a strong gaseous outflow.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mkn 938 as a Seyfert, gas-rich merger with central star formation and nuclear activity, mapping stellar and gas kinematic subsystems plus Na D and ionized galactic winds.

quotes.quote: We present the results of a detailed study of the central part of the Seyfert galaxy Mkn 938. Observational data were obtained with the 6-m telescope of the Special Astrophysical Observatory of the Russian Academy of Sciences using integral-field spectrograph MPFS and a scanning Fabry--Perot interferometer. Mkn 938 is interesting for being a result of a merger of two gas-rich galaxies, and we observe the final stage of this interaction accompanied with an extremely powerful burst of star formation and nuclear activity. Our analysis of the kinematics of gas and stars revealed the presence of gas outflow in the circumnuclear region Mkn 938 with velocities ranging from 370 to 480 km s ^ 1 , and allowed us for the first time to map the high-velocity galactic wind in Na D absorption line on large spatial scale in this galaxy.
We classified the interacting galaxy Mkn 938 (= NGC 34 = VV 850), which has a peculiar morpho lo gy and active nucleus, as Sy 2 afan80 . This classification was later confirmed Dah85,ver86 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC34 as the sample's most advanced merger, with shells, streams, a 300-600 Myr merger-age peak, and massive young clusters.

quotes.quote: NGC34 has already reached the final coalescing phase. Morphologically, the galaxy shows stellar shells far from the central regions, and streams of stars and gas that has led to formation of lower mass clusters. The pronounced age peak at 600 Myr suggests that the final merger event has taken place between 300 and 600 Myr ago, in agreement with a spectroscopic analysis performed by ss2007 . It is during that rapid merging phase that the galaxy has formed some of the most massive YSCs observed in the local universe bastian13 .

The molecular gas conditions in the central region of NGC34 are consistent with being heated by the AGN rather than a nuclear starburst e.g. xu14 , whom report a rotating molecular gas disk of 200 pc in radius (corresponding to 16 px in our dataset). Young clusters with ages below 10 Myr are mainly associated with visible spiral structures in the inner region of the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 34 as a merger-remnant LIRG with a central starburst and AGN, finding a circumnuclear star-formation ring and AGN-driven nuclear outflow signatures in near-infrared gas emission.

quotes.quote: The galaxy NGC 34 (NGC 17, Mrk 938), z=0.0196 2006AJ....131..185R and at a distance of 82 Mpc ( H_0 = 67.4 km s^ 1 Mpc^ 1 ), is a local representative of the LIRGs class ( (L_ IR /L_ ) = 11.61 , 1992AA...255...87C ). It hosts a dominant central starburst (e.g.: 2006AA...457...61R,2007AJ....133.2132S,2008MNRAS.388..803R,2014MNRAS.443.1754D ) with inferred star formation rates (SFR) in the range 50--90  M_ yr^ 1 2005AA...434..149V,2004AA...421..115P .

Our main findings are that NGC 34 hosts an AGN and that the nuclear starburst is distributed in a circumnuclear star-formation ring with inner and outer radii of  60 and 180 pc, respectively, as revealed by maps of the [ Fe ii ] / Pa and H_ 2 / Br emission-line ratios, and corroborated by PCA Tomography analysis. The spatially resolved NIR diagnostic diagram of NGC 34 also identifies a circumnuclear structure dominated by processes related to the stellar radiation field and a nuclear region where [ Fe ii ] and H _2 emissions are enhanced relative to the hydrogen recombination lines. We estimate that the nuclear X-ray source can account for the central H _2 enhancement and conclude that [ Fe ii ] and H _2 emissions are due to a combination of photo-ionization by young stars, excitation by X-rays produced by the AGN and shocks. These emission lines show nuclear, broad, blue-shifted components that can be interpreted as nuclear outflows driven by the AGN.

sources.summary: The paper classifies NGC 0034 as a radio AGN, citing an offset between 1.49 and 8.44 GHz radio peaks, a matching 8.44 GHz bulge position, and steep spectral-index values.

quotes.quote: NGC 0034 This is a galaxy pair, but only the south companion has been observed at both frequencies used in our analysis. The centre of radio emission at 1.49 GHz does not coincide with the HST bulge position haan11 , but is 0.8 arcsec away. The centre of the 8.44-GHz emission is matching the HST bulge position. The maximum flux pixel in the 1.49-GHz map is not at the same R.A. and Dec and the maximum flux pixel in the 8.44-GHz map. This offset is of the order of 1 arcsec, which is larger than the pixel size of the 1.49-GHz image (0.4 arcsec). We classify this object as a radio-AGN because of the shift, and because the radio spectral index in the map exceeds out theoretical value of 1.1.

sources.summary: NGC 0034 is a Seyfert 2 with hard X-ray excess, absorbed-AGN modeling, prior XMM-Newton AGN evidence, and NuSTAR-confirmed heavily obscured activity.

quotes.quote: [ 82 ] NGC 0034 : This source, optically classified as a Seyfert 2 e.g. VeiKim1999, YuaKew2010 , has an X-ray spectrum that shows a hard band excess. Fitting an absorbed AGN with a fixed photon index of 1.8 gives an absorbing column density of N_ H 1 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 . Previous analyses of XMM-Newton data confirm the presence of an AGN, be it through marginal detection of the Fe K line or modeling of an absorption or reflection component e.g. ShuWan2007, BriNan2011 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 34 as a stage-D LIRG merger with a hard X-ray AGN, obscured nucleus, NuSTAR-derived column density, and measured X-ray luminosity.

quotes.quote: IRAS F00085--1223 (NGC 34): This is a LIRG with a single nucleus, assigned as a stage-D merger. We identify the source as the hard X-ray detected AGN. The XMM-Newton studies indicate that NGC 34 hosts an obscured AGN Guainazzi2005,Brightman2011a , and the recent NuSTAR studies estimate its absorption column density to be N_ H 5 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 Ricci2017bMNRAS,Mingozzi2018 . This is consistent with our value of N_ H = (5.0 1.0) 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 by using the NuSTAR, Chandra, and XMM-Newton data. The absorption-corrected 2--10 keV luminosity we obtain is log L_ 2-10 = 41.90 0.10 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Mark 938 as a merging galaxy with tidal tails and a controversial transition spectrum resolved into star-forming and Seyfert 2 components.

quotes.quote: Mark 938 . This galaxy is apparently undergoing a merger as evidenced by the presence of tidal tails (Mulchaey et al. 1996; Mazzarella Boroson 1993). The nature of its emission-line spectrum has been rather controversial. Afa nasjev et al. (1980) classified it as a Seyfert 2, Osterbrock Dahari (1983) claimed that it is not a Seyfert; Dahari (1985), V eron-Cetty V eron (1986a) and Veilleux et al. (1995) called it again a Seyfert 2, while Mulchaey et al.

sources.summary: The paper finds the central 230 pc of NGC 34 dominated by a solar-metallicity, young-intermediate stellar population, consistent with its young disk, clusters, and CN absorption.

quotes.quote: NGC 34 The optical properties of NGC 34 (Mrk 938, MCG-02-01-032) are discussed in detail in the recent work of sch07 . Using photometry and Lick indices (when spectroscopy was possible) and comparing them with the models of bc03 they conclude, that NGC 34 has a young stellar disk and supports a rich system of young star clusters. In fact, as can be observed in Figs. popspec and pop and in Tab. sps , the light at 1.2230 \ in the 230 central pc of NGC 34 is also dominated by a stellar population with solar metallicity and a young-intermediate aged population ( 1 Gyr). These findings are in full agreement with the fact that NGC 34 has in its spectrum a strong 1.1 \ CN absorption band riffel07 characteristic of stellar populations with that age (M05).

sources.summary: The paper excludes NGC 34 from the reliable sample because X-ray interpretations disagree between Compton-thin and Compton-thick obscuration, while MIR data may be star-formation contaminated.

quotes.quote: NGC 34 Unfortunately, only one X-ray observation has been analyzed an published so far for NGC 34. and model the object as highly obscured (but Compton-thin), while claim that the obscuration is Compton-thick. The latter is support by the low X-ray-- ratio but on the other hand no Fe K line was detected. argue that the nucleus of NGC 34 is instead starburst dominated which is consistent with the MIR data (see also asmus_subarcsecond_2014 ). Therefore, NGC 34 is excluded from the reliable sample. Owing to the possible star formation contamination of the subarcsecond MIR luminosity, the prediction for from MIR--X-ray correlation is not reliable.

sources.summary: The atlas treats NGC 34 as an individually observed active or starburst galaxy, summarizing its prior classification and near-infrared continuum, emission-line, and absorption properties.

quotes.quote: NGC 34 . This is an infrared-luminous galaxy in an advanced stage of a merger, with two nuclei separated by approximately 6 kpc aam00 . It contains the most distant and one of the most luminous water vapor megamasers so far observed in a Seyfert galaxy hen05 . The nature of its emission-line spectrum is highly controversial. od83 claim that NGC 34 is an emission-line galaxy, but not a Seyfert one. based on the optical emission-line ratios. However, Veron-Cetty and Veron (1986) classify it as a Seyfert 2. aam00 argue that this galaxy is more properly classified as a starburst rather than a Seyfert.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Mrk 938 as highly inclined, with very red long-wavelength continua dominated by cold dust and starburst contribution that leaves excess 6.2 and 7.7 micron emission.

quotes.quote: Mrk 938 and NGC 3079 show very red long-wavelength continua in our sample, and both of these galaxies are highly inclined to our line of sight (see Table tab:objdata ), their continua are dominated by the emission from the cold dust.
This conclusion is supported by objects that show very red long-wavelength continua (NGC 3079 and Mrk 938).

On the other hand, in sources clearly dominated by starburst contribution such as Mrk 938 and NGC 3079, we find an excess of emission at 6.2 and 7.7 , after PAH bands at 11.3 and 17 are cleanly subtracted.

sources.summary: The atlas gives NGC 34's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: NGC 34 is an infrared-luminous late-stage merger system (nuclei separation 17 ; 6 kpc; PA 30 ; see schweizer_remnant_2007 for a detailed study) at a redshift of z= 0.0196 ( D 77 Mpc) with an active nucleus controversially classified either as a Sy 2 or H II mazzarella_optical_1993 . The nucleus is resolved and bright at radio wavelengths (PA 125 ; nagar_radio_1999 ), while the emission is weak and rather decentralized favouring a non-AGN nucleus ( mulchaey_emission-line_1996 ; see also riffel_0.8-2.4_2006 ). However, the most recent classification by is Sy 2 (see also goncalves_agns_1999 ), while X-ray observations revealed the presence of a Compton-thick obscured AGN shu_investigating_2007 , which might explain the previous contradicting results.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 34 as a merger remnant with unusually high nuclear luminosity density, redder central colors likely from dust, and possible ongoing or recent star formation.

quotes.quote: While most of the galaxies in our sample follow the same approximate correlation as normal ellipticals, there are three galaxies that strongly stand out because of their high luminosity density: NGC 34, 3921 and 7252. One other galaxy that stands out for the same reason is NGC 1316 from the F97 sample, which is also a merger remnant (Schweizer 1980). The J-H and H-K color profiles of NGC 3921 and 7252 show that they become bluer towards the center, presumably due to recent star formation. This is consistent with the detection of strong Balmer absorption lines in ground-based spectra of these galaxies (KSB98). NGC 34 becomes redder towards the center, probably as a result of dust absorption. NGC 34 is the most IR-luminous galaxy of those that we observed, suggesting the presence of ongoing or recent star formation in this galaxy as well.

gmw_00000153.name: NGC 235A
gmw_00000153.overview: NGC 235A is a peculiar lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1809.04788] and early-type spiral [arXiv:1310.2770] that contains a double nucleus [arXiv:1809.04788] and hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2112.07084, arXiv:1310.2770]. It is interacting with the nearby galaxy NGC 232 [arXiv:1809.04788, arXiv:2107.12529] and belongs to a compact group of four objects [arXiv:1809.04788].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 235 as NGC 232’s comparable-size companion, a peculiar S0 with a double nucleus, group membership, interacting debris, and similar blue luminosity and diameter.

quotes.quote: Although the companion galaxy to the NE, NGC 235 (ESO 474-G016, Fig. fig1 ), might be an evolved merger by itself because it has a double nucleus, the separation to NGC 232 and the lack of disturbed features may indicate that from the point of view of NGC 232 it is in an earlier stage of interaction as well. NGC 235 is a peculiar S0 which is separated by 2 \ (or 50 kpc) and with a (projected) velocity difference of 120 \ with respect to NGC 232.

Both NGC 232 and 235 are members of a southern compact group of four objects (SCG 10, or SCG 0040-2350, 1994AJ....107.1235P,2000AJ....120...47C,2002AJ....124.2471I ), although we note that two of them are probably merging and are both associated with NGC 235 itself and the fourth is an edge-on spiral galaxy (NGC 230, V = 6759 ) to the SW separated by about 5 9 ( 148 kpc) from NGC 232 and with no signs of interaction. On the other hand there is a signature of interacting debris between NGC 232 and NGC 235 from the H knot that is lying between the two galaxies 1994AJ....107...99R,2002ApJS..143...47D . These two objects are of comparable size. Assuming the same distance, the blue-band optical luminosities for NGC 232 and NGC 235 are L_ B = 2.8 10 ^ 10 L _ and 3.5 10 ^ 10 L _ . The optical diameters of NGC 232 and NGC 235 are comparable, with that of NGC 235 being slightly larger (24.4 kpc vs 34.8 kpc).

sources.summary: The survey notes NGC 235 as NGC 232’s interacting companion, with its own minor pair and detected CO, [C I], and dust continuum emission with differing line profiles.

quotes.quote: NGC 232, NGC 235, and PGC 2570 NGC 232 interacts with NGC 235, which is located at 53 kpc northeast, with a velocity offset of approximately 123 km s ^ 1 Larson_2016, Espada_2018 . In addition, NGC 235 had a minor interaction pair (PGC 2570). In NGC 232 and NGC 235, CO (4--3), [ C 1 ] (1--0), and dust continuum were detected. In NGC 235, CO (4--3) emission was detected only at the blue-shifted components (approximately 250 km s ^ 1 with a systematic velocity of approximately 123 km s ^ 1 ). Conversely, the [ C 1 ] (1--0) emission has a double peak in both the red- and blue-shifted components.

sources.summary: The paper identifies a newly confirmed AGN-ionized EELR in the merging NGC 235 system, detects part of it in archival HST imaging, and finds only tentative evidence that its AGN faded.

quotes.quote: We identified two new distant AGN-ionized EELR clouds, near NGC 235 and NGC 5514, and recovered the previously-known cloud in NGC 7252 NGC7252 . The feature in NGC 235 is marginally shown (in a single contour) by , but not discussed; they analyze the [ O III ] blobs 10 SW of the nucleus.
Fig. fig-ngc0235 shows the cloud in the NGC 235 system, including detection of a portion of the structure in an archival HST image.

Energy balance indicates that the AGN in NGC 5514 has likely faded by at least a factor 3 over the light-travel time difference of order 250,000 years; the AGN in NGC 235 may have faded but the uncertainties are too large to make a strong statement.

sources.summary: The atlas gives NGC 235A's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: NGC 235A is an early-type spiral galaxy in an interacting pair at a redshift of z= 0.0222 ( D 88.7 Mpc) with an AGN controversially classified either as Sy 1.0 maia_new_1987,veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 or Sy 2 (e.g., keel_seyfert_1996, tueller_swift_2008 ), see the discussion in . The fact that the nucleus is highly obscured in X-rays favours the Sy 2 classification also adopted winter_x-ray_2009 . It features extended emission in east-west direction ( 9 3.7 kpc; PA 80 ; mulchaey_emission-line_1996 ) with a slightly different radio extension (PA 43 ; nagar_radio_1999 ).

gmw_00000154.name: NGC 424
gmw_00000154.overview: NGC 424 is an inclined spiral galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770] classified as a Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1408.5414, arXiv:1310.2770] that exhibits polarized broad lines [arXiv:1505.01166, arXiv:1310.2770]. The nucleus is heavily obscured by Compton-thick material [arXiv:1408.5414, arXiv:1906.08824] and features a compact narrow-line region [arXiv:2010.12595] alongside extended radio structure [arXiv:1505.01166].

sources.summary: NuSTAR and archival spectra show NGC 424 is a luminous Seyfert 2 AGN with Compton-thick obscuration, reflection-dominated hard X-rays, and energetics disfavoring a directly observed absorbed continuum.

quotes.quote: We present X-ray spectral analyses for three Seyfert 2 active galactic nuclei, NGC 424, NGC 1320, and IC 2560, observed by in the 3--79 keV band. The high quality hard X-ray spectra allow detailed modeling of the Compton reflection component for the first time in these sources. Using quasi-simultaneous and data, as well as archival data, we find that all three nuclei are obscured by Compton-thick material with column densities in excess of 5 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 , and that their X-ray spectra above 3 keV are dominated by reflection of the intrinsic continuum on Compton-thick material. Due to the very high obscuration, absorbed intrinsic continuum components are not formally required by the data in any of the sources. We constrain the intrinsic photon indices and the column density of the reflecting medium through the shape of the reflection spectra.

NGC 424 is the most luminous of the three AGN in our sample. One of the spectral models that fully accounts for the features in its joint and spectrum is a two-component model consisting of an absorbed power law in addition to the reflected component. However, simple consideration of energetics essentially rules out the possibility that the absorbed power law represents the intrinsic continuum observed directly, absorbed along the line of sight to the nucleus.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 424 among local Compton-thick AGN and finds a luminous, low-covering-factor source whose column and opening-angle fits vary by model components.

quotes.quote: Our sample consists of 10 local ( z<0.1 ) CT AGN observed by : NGC 424, NGC 1068, 2MFGC 2280, NGC 1320, NGC 1386, NGC 3079, IC 2560, Mrk 34, NGC 4945 and the Circinus galaxy. With the exception of 2MFGC 2280, these sources were selected to be known CT AGN and to cover a wide range in X-ray luminosity. Particular attention was paid to selecting sources in the low ( 10^ 42 ) and high ( 10^ 44 ) luminosity regimes, thus enveloping the bulk of the local CT AGN population, which is at moderate luminosities, so that our sample could be as representative as possible, despite its small size.

parameter is consistent within the uncertainties for all of these except NGC 424. For this source, \ measures =4.6^ +1.3 _ 0.7 10^ 24 \ and \ measures =2.1^ +0.6 _ 0.3 10^ 24 . Allowing for the \ to vary like this does not however change our result on .

For NGC 424 the addition of pexrav to the fit causes a shift in \ of 25 . While the change in \ is large for NGC 424, when considering the sample as a whole we conclude that the addition of this components does not appear to introduce a systematic effect in the determination of .

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 424 as a nearby Compton-thick AGN test case for RXTorus, fitting XMM-Newton and NuSTAR spectra and finding a heavily obscured, very soft X-ray source.

quotes.quote: We applied RXTorus to the broadband X-ray spectrum of NGC 424 . This source was selected because, at a luminosity distance of d_ L =45.6 Mpc, it is one of the closest CT AGN detected by Swift/BAT Ricci2015 and observed by NuSTAR. We analyzed the XMM-Newton EPIC spectrum of the source using an 8.2 ks observation (ID 0002942301, PI M. Guainazzi). The source was observed by NuSTAR for 15.5 ks (ID 60061007002) as a part of the campaign aimed at following up AGN detected by Swift/BAT.

Both models find that the X-ray source of NGC 424 is heavily obscured and has a very soft primary X-ray emission, which is in agreement with previous studies carried out using NuSTAR data ( Balokovic2014 and Brightman2015 ). The values of the inclination angle and of R/ obtained with the two models are significantly different. The fit carried out with the atomic model results in a lower value of the inclination angle and of the ratio of the minor to major axis of the torus (i.e. a larger covering factor). The hydrogen column density along the line of sight is also different in the two cases, with values of 2.96^ +0.40 _ 0.51 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 and 1.45^ +0.36 _ 0.41 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 in the non-atomic and atomic configurations respectively.

sources.summary: The paper describes optical and NIR spectroscopy of NGC 424, notes broad coronal-line features, and concludes its narrow-line region is compact on scales below about 400 pc.

quotes.quote: Below, we detailed the observations and data reduction procedure for ESO 138-G001 and NGC 424. Goodman/SOAR Observations Optical spectra of ESO 138-G001 and NGC 424 were obtained with the 4.1 m Southern Observatory for Astrophysical Research (SOAR) Telescope at Cerro Pachon, Chile.
In NGC 424, broad features are also observed in the CLs. However, in ESO 138-G001, Mrk 1388 and J124134.25+442639.2 no evidence of broad CL were found.
For the closest objects (ESO 138-G 001, NGC 424, Mrk 1388 and III Zw 77), it translates to scales down to 400 pc. Therefore, the NLR of these objects should be very compact.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 424 as a type 2 Seyfert with polarized broad lines, extended radio structure, possible mid-infrared variability, and a Compton-thick X-ray source.

quotes.quote: NGC 424 NGC 424 was optically classified as a type 2 Seyfert galaxy smith1975 , and broad lines have been detected in polarized light moran2000 . At radio frequencies, it was observed with VLA at 6 and 20 cm, showing an extended structure ulvestadwilson1989 . A possible mid-IR variability was reported by between 2007 and 2009, but it could also be due to an ``observational inaccuracy''. At X-rays, it is a Compton thick source balokovic2014 . It was observed twice with XMM -Newton in 2008 and 2011, and once with Chandra in 2002.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC424 as a previously fitted NuSTAR source, one of the reflection-dominated AGN, with a moderate Compton-thick column density.

quotes.quote: For many other sources, e.g. NGC1068, Circinus, NGC424, NGC3079, NGC4945, NGC5643, NuSTAR spectral fits have been presented elsewhere bauer2015, arevalo2014, balokovic2014, puccetti2016, koss2015, puccetti2014, annuar2015, marchesi2018 . In general, our results are consistent with the work cited above. Four sources appear to be reflection dominated as the reflection radiation fraction is near unity. These are NGC1068, Circinus, NGC424 and NGC5643. There is not a clear correlation between the strength of the reflection component and the column density in these sources. For example, NGC424 and NGC5643 have 'moderate' Compton-thick column densities 1.3 10^ 24 cm^ 2 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 424 as a broad-line AGN in polarized light and uses its narrow [OIII] flux to estimate bolometric luminosity.

quotes.quote: NGC 424 : The broad H and H lines were detected in polarized emission by Moran2000 . They report an H FWZI of approximately 12,000 km/s. However, since the broad lines are not detected in unpolarized emission, we use the narrow-line [ OIII ] flux, F [ OIII ] = 4267.0 10 ^ 16 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 Gu2006 to derive the bolometric luminosity.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes X-ray spectra of NGC 424, describing its best-fit components, Fe K emission or flux behavior, and comparison with previous modeling.

quotes.quote: NGC 424 The default Chandra aperture extraction spectrum and XMM-Newton spectra were consistent and fit simultaneously using a double absorbed power law with a Gaussian component to accommodate the Fe K emission; including a thermal component did not statistically significantly improve the fit. We note that fitting the 3 - 8 keV continuum with a power law plus a Gaussian led to a tighter constraint on the Fe K emission and a more realistic EW value. Matt et al.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 424 as a Compton-thick AGN whose spectrum shows silicate emission rather than absorption, a Type-2 result partly confirmed by PAHFIT and consistent with little dust extinction.

quotes.quote: While the mid-IR spectra for eight of the Compton-thick AGN do appear to be moderately extinguished, we also find that the spectral decomposition (using DecompIR ) of NGC 424 shows strong evidence for Si- emission ( S_ 9.7 < 0.0 ); based on unified models, Si-emission features are expected only in unobscured Type-1 AGN, irrespective of specific torus geometry/composition. This result is also partially confirmed by PAHFIT which produces a relatively poor fit ( ^2_ n-1 26.1 ) for NGC 424 and by design does not allow apparent optical depths of S_ 9.7 < 0.0 .

At least eight of the sources exhibit little or no evidence for dust extinction ( S_ 9.7 0.1 ), and moreover, NGC 424 has silicate emission features ( S_ 9.7 < 0.0 ), in general, such emission features require a direct line-of-sight to the inner AGN torus.

sources.summary: The atlas gives NGC 424's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: NGC 424 is an inclined spiral galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.0118 ( D 49.5 Mpc) with an AGN controversially classified as a Sy 1 murayama_high-ionization_1998 or as Sy 2 smith_emission-line_1975 . Broad emission lines are detected in polarized light moran_frequency_2000 . The fact that the nucleus is highly obscured and probably even Compton-thick in X-rays favours the Sy 2 classification collinge_compton-thick_2000 .

sources.summary: NGC 424 is one of four bright, well-studied AGNs with previously detected parsec-scale elongated mid-infrared emission confirmed by the paper's Gaussian fits.

quotes.quote: Elongated mid-IR emission on the parsec-scale was found previously in four of the sources in this sample: NGC 424 2012ApJ...755..149H , NGC 3783 2013ApJ...771...87H , NGC 1068 2014A&A...565A..71L and the Circinus galaxy 2014A&A...563A..82T . These are among the brightest and very well studied sources ( 30 (u,v) points) where elongation and position angle can be constrained well. Our results (Tab. table:Tparelong ) are in excellent agreement with the published ones, confirming the reliability of the detected elongations.

sources.summary: For NGC 424, the paper finds that mytorus-based intrinsic luminosity estimates give bolometric corrections that better match established luminosity and Eddington-ratio relationships.

quotes.quote: However, for NGC 424 and NGC 3079 the \ values given the \ estimate from the mytorus model provide better agreement.
For our analysis of \ henceforth, we use the \ estimate from the torus model with the exception of NGC 424 and NGC 3079 where we use the \ estimate from the mytorus model.
We found that for NGC 424 and NGC 3079, the \ estimate from the mytorus model gave a \ value that is in better agreement with the relationships.

sources.summary: The paper follows a separate reduction approach for NGC 424, separating its strong nuclear emission and removing host-galaxy and nebular contamination from the AGN continuum.

quotes.quote: For NGC 424 we followed a different approach.
For sources with strong nuclear emission such as NGC 424, it was possible to accurately separate both components thanks to the method employed during the observations.
It is possible to verify in the bottom panel of Figure fig: spitz the removal of the NGC 424 host galaxy contribution and nebular emission, resulting in a pure continuum AGN emission.
